Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## AN ATTENTION FREE TRANSFORMER

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We introduce Attention Free Transformer (AFT), an efficient variant of Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) that eliminates the need for dot product attention. AFT offers great simplicity and efficiency compared with standard Transformers, where the multi-head attention operation is replaced with the composition of element-wise multiplications/divisions and global/local pooling. During training time, AFT has linear time and space complexity w.r.t. both the sequence length and feature dimension; in the autoregressive decoding mode, AFT has constant memory and time complexity per step. We show that, surprisingly, we are able to train AFT effectively on challenging benchmarks, and also to match or surpass the standard Transformer counterparts and other efficient variants. In particular, AFT achieves the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 autoregressive modeling with much reduced complexity, and also outperforms several efficient Transformer variants on Enwik8.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Attention mechanisms, represented by Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), have driven the advancement of various machine learning problems, including language modeling (Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al.), image modeling (Chen et al.), and set modeling (Lee et al., 2019). Different from other well known model architectures such as Convolutional Neural Nets (CNNs) or Recurrent Neural Nets (RNNs), Transformers enable direct interaction between every pair of elements within a sequence, which makes them especially powerful at capturing long term dependencies.

However, Transformers require high computational costs. The root cause of this challenge is the need to perform attention operations that have quadratic time and space complexity w.r.t the context size. This makes it especially difficult for Transformers to scale to inputs with large context sizes. A number of recent works have been dedicated to addressing the scalability issue of Transformers (Child et al., 2019; Kitaev et al., 2020; Rae et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020b; Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2020a; Choromanski et al., 2020). While the techniques adopted in the literature range from sparsity, locality sensitive hashing, low rank decomposition, kernel approximation and etc., most of them are trying to approximate the full attention operation.

In this paper, we take a bolder step towards the same goal, by proposing a computational module that does not use or approximate the standard dot product attention. We hence name our model the attention free transformer (AFT). Similar to dot product attention, AFT is composed of the interaction of three quantities, namely the query, key and value. What’s different, however, is that AFT operates solely based on element-wise operations. To be more concrete, they key and value are first multiplied element-wise, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension (in the causal model, this corresponds to a cumulative sum). The query is then multiplied with the reduced key-value representation element-wise to produce the final output. See Figure 1a for an illustration.

AFT maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence (up to proper masking). However, the computational cost is drastically reduced to a _O_ ( _Td_ ) complexity for time and space, where _T, d_ are the context length and feature dimension, respectively. In the autoregressive decoding mode, AFT also provides constant decoding time and space complexity per step, compared to _O_ ( _T_ ) for standard transformers. To the best of our knowledge, AFT is the first model that achieves such efficiency in the context of Transformers. See Table 1 for the complexity analysis of AFT in comparison to other variants.

We show that we can interpret AFT as an extreme case of multi head dot product attention (MHA). In particular, we show that by 1) setting the number of heads equal to the feature dimension in MHA and 2) using _relu_ in place of _softmax_ as the non-linearity, MHA can be decomposed into the summation of two AFT modules (see Equation 6). However, this relationship is not true in a general sense, i.e., by varying the non-linearity injected after the query and key in AFT, we can obtain models that do not have a MHA counterpart. This realization allows us to freely explore the design choices (e.g., nonlinearity) of AFT to achieve the best performance. This philosophy is in direct contrast with previous and concurrent “linearized attention” works (Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Choromanski et al., 2020), which are constrained by the design space of MHA.

We perform experiments with AFT on several benchmarks, including unconditional image modeling, image super-resolution, language modeling, machine translation and point cloud generation. We show that AFT works very well as an alternative to the standard Transformer, providing competitive results as well as excellent efficiency.

To summarize, our contributions are as follows:

- We propose AFT, a new family of Transformer models that achieves _O_ ( _Td_ ) time and space complexity in training, as well as _O_ ( _d_ ) time and space complexity in autoregressive decoding.

- We show strong performance of AFT as a drop in replacement of MHA on various benchmarks, including setting the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 in the standard setting and outperforming other efficient Transformer variants.

At the core of Transformers is the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) operation. Given three sequences, namely the query _Q ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ , key _K ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ and value _V ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ , and the number of heads _h_ , MHA performs a scaled dot product attention for each head _i_ , defined as:

where _Wi[Q] ∈ R[d][×][d][k]_ , _Wi[K] ∈ R[d][×][d][k]_ , _Wi[V] ∈ R[d][×][d][v]_ are linear transformations for head _i_ , and _σ_ is the non-linearity by default set as the _softmaxr_ function (subscript _r_ indicates softmax is applied to each row of a matrix). _dk, dv_ are dimensions for key and value, respectively. MHA concatenates the output of _h_ attention heads along the channel dimension, resulting in feature dimension _hdv_ . Unless otherwise mentioned, we assume _dk_ = _dv_ and _h_ = _ddk_[.][This means the query, key and value] are the same dimension within each head, and output dimension matches that of the input.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

## 3.1 ATTENTION FREE TRANSFORMER

We now define Attention free transformer (AFT), which provides an alternative to MHA. Given _Q, K, V_ , AFT first linearly transforms them into _Q[′]_ = _QW[Q]_ , _K[′]_ = _KW[K]_ , _V[′]_ = _V W[V]_ , then

where _⊙_ is the element-wise product, with support for broadcasting when the operands’ dimensions don’t exactly match[1] ; _σq, σk_ are nonlinearities applied to the query and key, respectively. Explained in words, the key and value are first combined with an element-wise multiplication, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension, yielding a fixed length context vector _∈ R[d]_ . This context vector is then multiplied with each row of the query, which forms the final output of an AFT layer.

One particularly useful variant of MHA is masked attention, oftentimes presented in the form of causal attention. Specifically, in auto-regressive models, queries are constrained to not be able to interact with keys and values beyond the curret position. In standard attention, this is usually implemented with an explicit binary masking matrix of shape _T ×T_ , with non-causal entries masked as 0. We show that it is also straightforward to extend AFT to the causal mode while maintaining its efficiency. We denote an AFT layer’s output as _Yt_ = _f_ ( _Q≤t, K≤t, V≤t_ ) _, t_ = 1 _, ..., T_[2] . **Discussions:** The design philosophy of AFT is to promote extreme efficiency, while keeping the benefit of standard Transformers. Concretely, AFT enables _direct_ interaction of any two elements within the sequence, which is arguably the biggest advantage of Transformers over other types of models such as RNNs and ConvNets. However, AFT gets rid of the need of performing the costly spatial dot product attention, by computing a reduced value representation with the weights only depending on the keys. The resulting operation has an extremely efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) w.r.t. both time and space, which is the first model that achieves **linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions** . Moreover, the causal mode of AFT has an additional advantage of a constant decoding cost per step, similar to (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). To see this, from Equation 3, we have a simple recursion of _Yt_ = _σq_ ( _Q[′] t_[)] _[⊙]_[(] _[σ][k]_[(] _[K] t[′]_[)] _[⊙][V] t[′]_[+] _[KV][t][−]_[1][)][ with] _[ KV][t]_[=][ �] _[t] t[′]_ =1 � _σk_ ( _Kt[′][′]_[)] _[⊙][V] t[′][′]_ �, assuming _σq, σk_ are both element-wise functions. One thus only need to keep _KVt_ in memory, and update it with constant cost per step.

**Selecting nonlinearies:** _σq, σk_ provide additional nonlinearity which helps to increase model’s capacity. Empirically, we have found that one particularly strong setting is to let _σk_ = _softmax_ which is normalized along the context dimension. This choice brings an interesting benefit especially in the causal mode, which we can explicitly write as:

Here _gt_ ( _t_ ) acts as a role similar to that of an input gate in an LSTM, and _gt_ ( _t[′]_ ) is operating like the forget gate, which depends on the input of time _t_ , dynamically downweights the contribution of past time steps. When augmented with standard position embeddings as commonly used in Transformers, this allows the model to be able to learn the notion of recency at the same time of having access to the full context in the history. From this view, _σq_ can also be interpreted as the output gate, for which we found that both _sigmoid_ and _relu_ work well, with the former being slightly better. Also note that the same space and time complexity still holds for _σk_ = _softmax_ , both in training and decoding. In our experiments, unless otherwise mentioned, we use the _sigmoid_ + _softmax_ setting for _σq_ and _σk_ by default.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Since the Transformer was introduced, there have been numerous attempts to address the major source of inefficiency in the architecture, the quadratic cost of the attention operation. Improving this

**Approximating the dot product** . Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) both propose to approximate the exponential kernel with inner product of projections, which leads to a linearized attention operation of complexity _O_ ( _Td_[2] ). AFT is similar but offers greater efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) due to the exclusive use of element-wise operations, as well as more design flexibility. Reformers (Kitaev et al., 2020) apply LSH as an approximation to the dot product, whereas AFT completely gets rid the need of dot product.

**Sparse, local attention** . Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019) and Image Transformer (Parmar et al., 2018) proposes to use fixed sparse or local context patterns. Attention models in vision tasks (often combined with convolutions) use image structure to help handcraft relevant spatial patterns to attend (Wang et al., 2020a; Huang et al., 2019b; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019a; Ramachandran et al., 2019). AFT also borrows the locality idea, but we put it as a bias rather than hard constraint (see AFT-local-learned) . Also AFT is a standalone module, where it works as a plug in replacement of MHA in autoregressive tasks.

**Context compression** . Other approaches try to learn context patterns. Adaptive-Span Transformers (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019) learn a range for each attention head within which to attend. Routing transformers (Roy et al., 2020) use clustering to compute dot-product attention only over a subset of elements within the same cluster. The Linformer (Wang et al., 2020b) reduces the length of the context by compressing the keys and values with a linear layer. Compressive Transformers (Rae et al., 2020) compute and update reduced representations of the input that are far enough back in the input sequence, and attend to those compressed representations. AFT is largely complementary to these approaches, as our focus is to improve the complexity of any given sequence from the operation level.

**Eliminating dot product attention** . Instead of limiting the number of comparisons, other methods change the operation used to compute attention. The Synthesizer (Tay et al., 2020a) uses attention weights predicted from inputs, rather than derived from dot-product interactions. The LightConv module introduced in (Wu et al., 2019) proposes to replace the dot product self-attention with dynamic lightweight depthwise convolution, where the weights are normalized across temporal dimension. The Sinkhorn Transformer (Tay et al., 2020b) uses a differentiable sorting operation to identify relevant comparisons that may not be local in the original sequence order. AFT can be viewed as a more drastic version in this direction, where the we adopt a single global ”attention mask” ( _w_ ) of all ones (vanilla AFT) or with a few learnable entries (AFT-local-learned).

**Gated RNNs** . AFT is also related to the classic line of work on gated RNN variants, including LSTMs Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997), GRUs (Chung et al., 2014) and QuasiRNNs (Bradbury et al., 2016). AFT maintains the benefit of RNN models (linear complexity w.r.t. sequence length, constant decoding cost), but offers great parallelism and effectiveness, thanks to the use of a simple context reduction operation, which is amendable to a fully parallel implementation during training. We believe that AFT also offers new perspectives for rethinking the success and limitations of gated RNNs.

**Dynamic Convolution** . AFT is also related to dynamic convolution (Wu et al., 2019) when applied to auto-regressive tasks, where the reduced key-value representation can be interpreted as a per sequence convolutional kernel. However, AFT operates in an extreme case where the dimension of the kernel is 1 along both the feature and spatial dimensions, again presenting superior efficiency.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct experiments on five tasks: unconditional image modeling (Sec. 5.2), language modeling (Sec. 5.3), machine translation (Sec. 5.4), image super resolution (Sec. B.2) and point cloud generation (Sec. B.3). We focus on the causal mode of AFT, while leaving systematic evaluation of the non-causal version as future work. Unless otherwise mentioned, all experiments are conducted on 8 _×_ V100 GPU machines.

## 5.1 EFFICIENCY

To support our analysis in Table 1, we benchmarked an implementation of AFT on a single forward and backward pass on random data with a batch size of 4. We compared AFT with the self-attention from Transformers, a linear attention mechanism from (Katharopoulos et al., 2020), and a Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020). For all of these, we used a base architecture with 12 layers, 8 heads (except for AFT, where there are no heads), feature dimension of either 256 or 1024, and context lengths up to 10,000. We used the code from the fast transformers library to perform the evaluations[3] . Results in terms of runtime and peak GPU usage are in Figure 2. Where data-points do not exist in the figure, the model exhausted GPU memory. We see from this that compared to Transformers and Reformers, AFT and linear attention require far fewer computational resources as context increases. In addition, we see that AFT is not as sensitive to the feature dimension as the linear attention, which expands the design space for feasible models. In all settings, AFT performs best. ## 5.2 UNCONDITIONAL IMAGE MODELING

In our first set of experiments, we consider the problem of image modeling by minimizing the negative log likelihood (NLL). Similar to Parmar et al. (2018), we represent an RGB image as a sequence of length _H × W ×_ 3, with _H, W_ being the height and width, respectively. Each sub-pixel is represented as a 256-way discrete variable. We use CIFAR10 for image density modeling.

**Feasibility study and choice of nonlinearities.** We first conduct experiments validating the legitimacy of AFT and its four nonlinearity variants. Our reference Transformer design largely follows that of Chen et al., where a transformer block consists of an attention layer (AFT layer in our cae) with residual connection and a 2 layer MLP with residual connections. Layer Normalization (LN) (Ba et al., 2016) is applied in a “pre-act” fashion. We adopt learned position embeddings, and use a set of shared token embeddings and prediction heads across RGB.

Our base architecture consists of 24 Transformer blocks, each with d=256 dimensional features. The hidden layer of the MLP per block has 4 dimensionality of its input. We use Adam, and follow a standard warmup learning rate schedule as in Vaswani et al. (2017). We use an initial learning rate of 3 _×_ 10 _[−]_[3] and a weight decay of 0 _._ 1 applied to all linear transformations weights, and a dropout of 0.1.

We adopt simple data augmentation. During training, we first randomly flip each image horizontally, then add or subtract a value in the range [ _−_ 10 _,_ 10] from all its subpixels, and clip resulting pixel values to [0 _,_ 255]. We use cross entropy loss, and a default batch size of 128 for 200 training epochs. We train three versions of AFT, namely AFT-relu2 (Equation 6), AFT-relu ( _σq_ = _σk_ = _relu_ ), AFT-relu-softmax: ( _σq_ = _relu_ , _σk_ = _softmax_ ), AFT-sigmoid-softmax ( _σq_ = _sigmoid_ , _σk_ = _softmax_ ), all of which use full contexts. We show the training and test loss curves in Figure 4. All versions of AFT are trainable with standard optimization techniques for Transformers. In particular, AFT-relu2 performs slightly worse than AFT-relu, and both are significantly worse than AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. AFT-sigmoid-softmax also outperforms the strong PixelCNN++ baseline (Salimans et al., 2017). Based on this observation, we use AFT-sigmoidsoftmax as the default setting for all remaining experiments, unless otherwise mentioned.
**Comparing with the state of the art.** CIFAR10 is a crowded benchmark for image autoregressive modeling, and we compare with a few competitive baselines, as shown in Table 2. Note that

AFT achieve the state-of-the-art result in this setting, with much fewer parameters, better speed and significantly less memory.

CIFAR10 has an unrolled sequence length of 3072, which is already prohibiting to train a full Transformer with reasonable size. For example, for a standard 12 layer 512 dimension and 8 head configuration, the maximum batch size we can fit in our 8 V100 node is only 16, which makes it infeasible already. Our closest baseline Image Transformer (Parmar et al., 2018), which restricts attention to local2d windows of size of 256. We test our AFT-local-learned and aft-local2d-hard variants, with the same window size, and the same architecture as well as a deeper but narrower one (24 layer and 256 dimensions), which are still fair comparisons. We also compare to Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019), which restrains attention to sparse but global subset of context elements.

From Table2, we see that all AFT variants outperform the Image Transformer baseline. Both AFT local versions are better than the full counterpart, with AFT-local-learned being significantly stronger than others. We also observe that the deeper but narrower architecture is more effective than the shallow but wide baseline. Our best model also achieves the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 in this setting, outperforming a much larger Sparse Transformer model. Efficiency wise, we benchmarked Image Transformer against AFT variants on a 8 V100 GPU node[4] . All our variants are faster than Image Transformer, while consuming only half of the memory[5] .

## 5.3 LANGUAGE MODELING

We apply AFT to character level language modeling on Enwik8 (Mahoney, 2011), which is another popular benchmark for auto-regressive modeling. We follow the standard preprocessing procedures and training/validation/test splits as in (Dai et al., 2019). Our base Transformer reference is a 12 layer 512 dimensional 8 head architecture with 2048 feed forward dimensions. For the first set of experiments, we use sequence length of 1024. Our training protocol as largely the same as before, except that we increase the weight decay to 0.5 and train for 100 epochs with batch size 128 in all experiments. We evaluate the AFT-local-learned variant with a window size of 32 and _du_ = 256. We also compare to several efficient Transformer baselines, namely Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020), Synthesizer (Tay et al., 2020a) and Linear Transformer (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). From Table 3, we see that with the base _L_ = 12 _, d_ = 512 architecture, AFT achieves the lowest training bits per character (bpc), indicating its high capacity. Its test performance is slightly worse than that of the basic Transformer, but outperforms all other three variants. The deeper and narrower architecture of AFT strikes the best balance across parameter, speed, memory and performance. Its test bpc is only 0.02 away from the full Transformer’s, while only consuming a third of the memory and provides a 44% speedup. In the end, we have also trained the same architecture with a sequence length of 2048, which results in an improved performance both on the training and test set. This suggests AFT’s ability to effectively model long range dependencies.

## 5.4 MACHINE TRANSLATION

As a machine translation benchmark, we show experiments with the WMT 2014 English to German translation task. The training set contains approximately 4.5 million sentence pairs. We compare against a Transformer architecture baseline using the OpenNMT implementation (Klein et al., 2017). For translation, the standard architecture is an encoder-decoder structure, where the encoder uses non-causal attention to encode the input sentence. The decoder uses two different types of attention. The first, self attention, sequentially attends to the output translation as it is being generated token by token. The second attends to the translation and the context from the encoder.

In our experiments, we replace the multi-headed decoder self-attention blocks. We compare perplexity (PPL), BLEU score, and efficiency between the Transformer base and AFT in Table 5. In this task, we see that AFT performs on par with the Transformer. As expected for the small context size, typically around 50 tokens, AFT does not show dramatic improvements in speed or memory.

Table 5: WMT 2014 English-to-German Translation.

|Method|TrainingPPL|Validation PPL|Test BLEU score|tokens/sec|GB/GPU|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Transformer|4.38|4.06|27.32|54.7K|7.80|
|AFT(Ours)|4.34|4.10|27.70|54.4K|7.54|

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

We have introduced the Attention Free Transformer that replaces attention with an efficient, easyto-implement new operation. We have demonstrated strong results on challenging benchmarks, despite of the simplicity of our design. We believe that our model opens a new design space for Transformer-like models, and will see impact in various areas where Transformers are applied.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated architecture that seeks to address the quadratic computational bottleneck of standard Transformers by replacing dot-product attention with element-wise interactions. The clarity of the exposition is excellent; the authors provide a clear intuition behind the mechanics of the "Attention Free Transformer" (AFT), effectively contrasting their design philosophy—which prioritizes architectural flexibility outside the constraints of kernel approximations—against existing "linearized" attention literature. The complexity analysis provided in Table 1 is particularly helpful and underscores the practical significance of the $O(Td)$ training and $O(d)$ inference scaling. The most significant potential issue for a reviewer will be the empirical robustness: while the theoretical contributions are strong, the paper must convincingly demonstrate that the removal of global dot-product attention does not sacrifice the expressive power required for downstream tasks, particularly in high-entropy language modeling contexts where long-range dependencies are notoriously sensitive to the exactness of the attention mechanism. Provided the experimental section maintains this rigor, the paper is well-positioned for publication.

The formula $h = dd_k$ is likely a garbled representation of $h = d/d_k$. progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript proposes a novel and ambitious variant of the Transformer, the Attention Free Transformer (AFT), which eliminates dot-product attention in favor of element-wise operations and pooling to achieve linear training complexity and constant decoding overhead. The work demonstrates high quality in its theoretical framing, particularly the insightful connection between AFT and Multi-Head Attention as an extreme case utilizing ReLU non-linearity and a head dimension equal to the feature dimension, which provides a compelling motivation for the architectural choice. The writing is clear and accessible, and the complexity analysis is presented effectively with a helpful comparison table. However, the most significant issue concerns the justification of AFT's expressive power; while the efficiency gains are attractive, the introduction lacks a rigorous argument for how the simplified interaction mechanism, which involves pooling the element-wise product of keys and values, can preserve the long-range dependency modeling capabilities of standard attention without suffering from severe information bottlenecks. Furthermore, the evaluation strategy requires careful scrutiny to ensure fair comparisons with concurrent efficient Transformer variants, specifically regarding the extent of hyperparameter tuning and training effort applied to baselines, as efficiency-performance trade-offs must be quantified precisely to substantiate the claim that AFT matches or surpasses SOTA results. Finally, the description of the pooling strategy (global vs. local) and associated hyperparameters is currently underspecified in the provided text, warranting more detailed exposition to assess reproducibility and the sensitivity of the method to design choices.

Because a context-wise softmax cannot be decomposed into a sum of independent per-time-step terms, the stated recursion _KVt = KVt-1 + σk(K'_)t ⊙ V'_t_ breaks down unless cumulative normalization tracking is introduced. comments.title: Overgeneralization of kernel approximation claims in Related Work
comments.quote: Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) both propose to approximate the exponential kernel with inner product of projections, which leads to a linearized attention operation of complexity O(Td_[2] ).
comments.explanation: Initially, I wondered if the authors meant to group these two works under a single kernel approximation paradigm. However, upon checking the literature, Katharopoulos et al. (2020) explicitly proposes approximating attention with *arbitrary* kernel functions $\phi$, while Choromanski et al. (2020) uses a low-rank approximation specifically for the softmax attention matrix. Characterizing both strictly as approximating the 'exponential kernel' is a technical inaccuracy that conflates their distinct mathematical approaches. The authors' broader point about linearization is valid, but the specific mechanistic claim is imprecise. Given this appears in the overview-focused Related Work section, it does not undermine the core methodology, but it should be corrected for academic rigor.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of the masking structure in Local AFT
comments.quote: where the we adopt a single global ”attention mask” ( w ) of all ones (vanilla AFT) or with a few learnable entries (AFT-local-learned).
comments.explanation: The text describes $w$ as a 'single global attention mask'. In the context of the paper and the defined notation $w_{t,t'}$, $w$ is actually a full $T \times T$ matrix (or tensor for 2D) of scalar weights/biases, not a single scalar value. The authors likely mean that vanilla AFT uses a uniform mask (all ones) rather than a position-dependent one. While the intent to contrast uniform vs. learnable locality biases is clear from the surrounding text and the summary's definition of $w_{t,t'}$, the phrase 'single global attention mask' could mislead a careful reader into expecting a scalar parameter or a fixed binary mask. Specifying that $w$ refers to the *pattern* or *values* of the mask matrix would be more precise.

comments.title: Syntactic break causing minor reading ambiguity in benchmark analysis
comments.quote: Figure 3, we see that the total runtime of AFT increases linearly with the context length, and is faster than linear attention and Transformers with self-attention.
comments.explanation: The sentence begins directly with 'Figure 3,' missing a preposition like 'In' or 'As shown in'. This grammatical slip causes a momentary parse ambiguity, making it unclear at first glance whether 'Figure 3' is the subject of the clause or a reference pointer. The technical claim itself is sound and consistent with the complexity analysis (constant $O(d)$ per step yields linear total time $O(Td)$ with respect to context length $T$). However, the structural error momentarily disrupts the logical flow and could cause a reader to misinterpret the subject of the observation. Adding a preposition would fully resolve the issue.

comments.title: Mismatched footnote citation for memory efficiency claim
comments.quote: while consuming only half of the memory[5] .
comments.explanation: The text claims AFT variants consume only half the memory of the Image Transformer baseline. However, the attached footnote [5] discusses the infeasibility of fairly comparing against Sparse Transformer due to implementation differences (mixed precision, gradient checkpointing). This is a clear copy-paste error where the citation marker was placed incorrectly. The memory claim itself is numerically accurate (9.5 GB vs 22.3 GB in Table 2), but the misplaced footnote introduces ambiguity about whether the memory saving applies to Image Transformer or Sparse Transformer.

comments.title: Highly suspicious weight decay hyperparameter
comments.quote: except that we increase the weight decay to 0.5 and train for 100 epochs with batch size 128 in all experiments.
comments.explanation: A weight decay value of 0.5 is extraordinarily high for transformer training. Standard practice uses values like 0.01 or 0.05. Given the authors previously used 0.1 for CIFAR10 and standard language modeling pipelines typically use small regularization, 0.5 is almost certainly a typographical error for 0.05. Taken literally, it would aggressively shrink weights to near-zero within a few steps, likely preventing convergence, which contradicts the reported successful training and low training bpc (0.854). This needs correction to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous MLP dimensionality specification
comments.quote: The hidden layer of the MLP per block has 4 dimensionality of its input.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'has 4 dimensionality of its input' is grammatically incorrect and mathematically ambiguous. The standard architecture uses an MLP expansion factor of 4 (i.e., $4d$ or 1024 features when $d=256$). The author likely meant 'has 4× the dimensionality of its input' or 'has 4 times its input dimensionality'. As written, it could mislead a reproducer about whether the hidden size is fixed at 4 or scales linearly.

comments.title: Slight overstatement of memory reduction
comments.quote: while only consuming a third of the memory and provides a 44% speedup.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the deeper/narrower AFT model consumes 'a third of the memory' compared to the base Transformer. Table 3 shows 11.2 GB vs 29.4 GB, which is approximately 38%. While 'a third' is a common rhetorical approximation, it is slightly optimistic. The 44% speedup claim is accurate ((2.04 - 1.42)/1.42 ≈ 43.7%). The memory claim is not a critical error but slightly overstates the efficiency gain.

comments.title: Terminology inconsistency: 'bpc' used for CIFAR-10 image modeling
comments.quote: we show the results on 4, where we see that both the training and testing bpc forms a U-shape w.r.t. the window size, with 32 achieving the best performance.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the use of 'bpc' (bits per character) in Section 5.3, which reports results for CIFAR-10 unconditional image modeling. The notation list explicitly defines `bpc: bits per character`, a metric appropriate for language modeling (e.g., Enwik8). For image density modeling on CIFAR-10, the standard and physically meaningful metric is bits per dimension (bits/dim) or bits per pixel. I checked Section 5.2 and the surrounding text to see if they redefined bpc or used pixel-level discretization, but the term remains unchanged. This is likely a terminology carryover from language modeling experiments. While the U-shape trend itself is clearly supported by the table data, the metric label creates unnecessary ambiguity for reproducibility and comparison with literature, where bits/dim is the expected unit for CIFAR-10.

comments.title: Missing reference word in text
comments.quote: We show the results on 4,
comments.explanation: My initial concern was that the sentence seemed to abruptly reference '4' without specifying what it referred to. I checked the surrounding context and immediately observed Table 4 presented directly after this sentence. Given the explicit mention of 'train bpc' and 'test bpc' and the window size sweep discussed in the same paragraph, it is virtually certain that 'on 4,' is a truncated or OCR-mangled reference to 'on Table 4,'. A careful reader can easily resolve this from context, so it does not impact the technical reasoning or reproducibility. However, the omission is a minor typesetting/OCR artifact that should be corrected for publication readiness to avoid momentary confusion.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an "Attention Free Transformer" (AFT) that significantly reduces computational complexity from quadratic to linear ($O(Td)$) by replacing dot-product attention with element-wise multiplications and pooling operations. The presentation is clear, and the authors make a compelling theoretical case distinguishing AFT from previous "linearized attention" methods by arguing that the removal of the softmax constraint and the specific pooling mechanism allow for a design space not constrained by standard Multi-Head Attention. A key strength is the strong comparison of computational efficiency provided in the introduction, highlighting that AFT achieves linear space and time complexity during

comments.title: Misleading total training complexity claim
comments.quote: During training time, AFT has linear time and space complexity w.r.t. both the sequence length and feature dimension; ... [Table 1] AFT(ours) Time @ train O(Td)
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly define the model operation as first linearly transforming $Q, K, V$ into $Q', K', V'$ via dense projections ($QW_Q$, $KW_K$, $VW_V$). For standard weight matrices of dimension $d 	imes d$, the cost of these projections is $O(Td^2)$. Adding this to the $O(Td)$ cost of the element-wise pooling operation results in a total training time complexity of $O(Td^2)$. The paper claims $O(Td)$ total complexity, which is mathematically inconsistent with the inclusion of standard linear projections unless the authors assume a specific non-standard (and unstated) structure for the weight matrices (e.g., diagonal or low-rank). This error fundamentally affects the core contribution regarding efficiency.

comments.title: Typo in case reference
comments.quote: AFT layer in our cae
comments.explanation: The text reads 'cae', which is clearly a typo for 'case'. This is a minor grammatical error that does not affect the mathematical or technical understanding of the methodology.

comments.title: Lowercase 'aft' inconsistent with Table header
comments.quote: AFT-local-learned and aft-local2d-hard variants
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'aft-local2d-hard' using a lowercase 'a', while the corresponding row in Table 2 is titled 'AFT-local2d-hard'. While this is a minor formatting inconsistency, it could confuse a reader scanning for table references.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reporting AFT-relu2 vs AFT-relu
comments.quote: In particular, AFT-relu2 performs slightly worse than AFT-relu, and both are significantly worse than AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax.
comments.explanation: Figure 4 caption states that 'AFT-relu2 and AFT-relu perform similarly'. There is a contradiction between the prose description (claiming AFT-relu2 performs *slightly worse*) and the figure caption (claiming they are similar). This discrepancy weakens the transparency of the result reporting.

comments.title: Minor Typo in Word Choice
comments.quote: additional experiments wit the _L_ = 24 _, d_ = 256 architecture
comments.explanation: The text uses 'wit' instead of 'with'. This is a clear OCR or spelling error that does not affect the mathematical understanding of the passage, but it is a distraction for the reader.

comments.title: Underspecified Model Architecture
comments.quote: We compare against a Transformer architecture baseline... We compare perplexity (PPL), BLEU score, and efficiency between the Transformer base and AFT in Table 5.
comments.explanation: While the text frames the comparison as a 'drop-in replacement' or comparison against a 'Transformer base', it does not explicitly state the specific depth (number of layers) and width (feature dimension $d$) used for the AFT model in Table 5, nor does it confirm that the AFT model used the 'deeper, narrower' architecture ($L=24, d=256$) preferred in the paper's summary for Image modeling, which might have roughly matched FLOPs to the standard Transformer. However, the mention of 'small context size' and the similar speed/efficiency metrics (Table 5) strongly implies an architecture-comparable baseline was used. This is a minor oversight in specification but likely does not undermine the validity of the comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous Phrasing Regarding Performance
comments.quote: In this task, we see that AFT performs on par with the Transformer.
comments.explanation: Table 5 reports a BLEU score of 27.70 for AFT compared to 27.32 for the Transformer. An improvement of 0.38 BLEU points (approximately 1.4% relative improvement) is statistically significant. The phrase 'performs on par' understates this specific achievement and could be misinterpreted by a reader as equality, whereas the data shows AFT yields a strictly better result.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Review Feedback

**Overall Assessment:** This paper presents a conceptually clean and computationally attractive alternative to the standard Transformer's multi-head attention mechanism. The core idea—replacing the quadratic-complexity dot-product attention with an element-wise operation that achieves linear complexity in both sequence length and feature dimension—is clearly motivated and well-articulated. The writing is generally clear and the theoretical complexity comparison in Table 1 is helpful for situating the contribution relative to prior work.

**Most Significant Issues:**

1. **Insufficient justification for effectiveness:** The paper claims that AFT "maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence," but this is asserted rather than rigorously demonstrated. The mechanism described—where key and value are combined element-wise and then pooled over the context dimension—does not obviously preserve the same pairwise interaction structure as standard attention. The authors do not provide an intuitive explanation or formal proof of how information flows between arbitrary positions, nor do they discuss what kinds of dependencies AFT might fail to capture compared to full attention.

2. **Missing details on the pooling operation:** The abstract mentions "global/local pooling," and the method section refers to a "cumulative sum" in the causal case, but the precise pooling mechanism (average vs. weighted vs. learned) is not fully specified in the provided excerpt. This ambiguity makes it difficult to assess whether the claimed linear complexity is achieved without sacrificing representational power in non-causal settings.

3. **Limited novelty framing:** The connection to multi-head attention (MHA) drawn in Section 3—showing AFT as an extreme case of MHA with specific hyperparameter and nonlinearity choices—is interesting, but this framing also risks diminishing the claimed novelty. The paper would benefit from more clearly distinguishing between "what is conceptually new" versus "what is a computationally efficient simplification of existing architectures." This is especially important given the crowded landscape of efficient Transformer variants.

comments.title: Overstated claim of 'direct interaction between any two elements'
comments.quote: AFT maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence (up to proper masking).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'direct interaction between any two elements' suggests that, like dot-product attention, AFT computes a separate weight for each query–key pair. In reality, AFT first pools keys (after non‑linearity) across the context into a single context vector per channel, then multiplies that vector element‑wise with the query. This gives each query the same aggregated context; there is no pairwise query‑key interaction. While any two positions can still influence each other (the value at one position enters the pooled vector and thus affects the query output at a later position), the mechanism is fundamentally different from the per‑pair weighting of standard attention. The qualifier 'up to proper masking' does not resolve this difference. A careful reader might infer that AFT preserves the pairwise attention weights, which it does not. This could be misleading when contrasting AFT with other efficient variants.

comments.title: Simplification of causal pooling as 'cumulative sum'
comments.quote: the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension (in the causal model, this corresponds to a cumulative sum).
comments.explanation: The description in the introduction says that after element‑wise multiplication of key and value, the result is 'pooled over the context dimension' and that in the causal model this corresponds to a cumulative sum. However, the actual AFT‑causal formulation (Equation 3 ff.) uses a softmax normalisation over the keys before pooling, so the operation is not a plain cumulative sum but rather a cumulative sum of exponentials (for the denominator) and a cumulative sum of exponentials times values (for the numerator). The text later clarifies this with the explicit formula, but the introductory sentence alone could give a reader the incorrect impression that no normalisation is involved. This is a minor imprecision.

comments.title: Missing definition of notation in recursion formula
comments.quote: we have a simple recursion of Yt = σq(Q′t) ⊙ (σk(K′t)⊙V′t + KVt−1) with KVt =  t t′ =1  σk(Kt′′)⊙Vt′′
comments.explanation: The recursion formula contains garbled characters (e.g., '') likely due to OCR corruption. The intended formula appears to be Y_t = σ_q(Q'_t) ⊙ (σ_k(K'_t)⊙V'_t + KV_{t-1}) with KV_t = Σ_{t'=1}^t (σ_k(K'_{t'}) ⊙ V'_{t'}). The explanation is understandable from context, but the corrupted symbols make it hard to verify the exact intended expression. Since this is likely OCR noise rather than author error, I consider it minor, but it could be confusing.

comments.title: Underspecified: 'pooled over the context dimension' in non-causal case
comments.quote: the key and value are first combined with an element-wise multiplication, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension, yielding a fixed length context vector ∈ R^d
comments.explanation: The term 'pooled over the context dimension' is ambiguous. Does this mean average pooling, sum pooling, or something else? In the causal case, they later specify a cumulative sum, but for the general (non-causal) case, the type of pooling is not specified. The summary mentions 'global/local pooling' but does not define the operation. A reader might not know whether to use sum, mean, or max pooling. The later equations (e.g., Equation 3) imply a summation, but earlier in the passage, the prose is vague. This could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Overclaim: 'first model that achieves linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions'
comments.quote: which is the first model that achieves linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions
comments.explanation: The authors claim AFT is the first model to achieve O(Td) complexity. However, the Linear Transformer (Katharopoulos et al., 2020) also achieves O(Td^2) which is linear in T but quadratic in d. The authors differentiate by noting that AFT is linear in d as well (O(Td) vs O(Td^2)). While this is technically true, the phrase 'first model' may be an overstatement if there exist other models (e.g., lightweight convolutions, gated RNNs) that also achieve O(Td) or better. The authors later acknowledge related work like gated RNNs, but the claim here is bold. Given that AFT is a Transformer variant, the claim may be defensible within that subfamily, but it is worth flagging as potentially overreaching.

comments.title: Missing definition of indicator function I(·) in locality bias
comments.quote: wt,t′ =  tt′ ex=1p([exp(] I(t−[I](t[t′] <s[−][t′]) [<s] uT[t)] [v]u tt[T′]) [v] t′[)][, where I(·) is an indicator function
comments.explanation: The formula for the learned local bias is heavily garbled by OCR, but from context it appears to define w_{t,t'} = exp(I(t-t' < s) u_t^T v_{t'}) / Σ_{t'=1}^t exp(I(t-t' < s) u_t^T v_{t'}). However, the numerator appears to include an unexplained term 'T' (possibly transpose) and the formatting is corrupted. Additionally, the denominator is not clearly shown. The intention is understandable, but the corruption could lead to misinterpretation. Since this is likely OCR error, I flag it as minor.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of AFT kernel dimension in Dynamic Convolution comparison
comments.quote: However, AFT operates in an extreme case where the dimension of the kernel is 1 along both the feature and spatial dimensions, again presenting superior efficiency.
comments.explanation: The authors attempt to relate AFT to dynamic convolution, but the description of the kernel's dimensions is incorrect. In AFT, the pooled key–value representation is a vector of dimension d (the feature dimension), not 1. Only the spatial dimension is reduced to 1 due to pooling. Claiming that both dimensions are 1 would imply the kernel is a scalar, which does not match AFT's element-wise multiplication of a d‑dimensional vector with the query. This mischaracterization could confuse readers about AFT's operation and its efficiency claims. The earlier description of AFT's complexity and operation elsewhere in the paper correctly implies a d‑dimensional kernel, so this is an internal inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Figure 4 caption and figure legend
comments.quote: Figure 4: Proof of concept experiments for AFT-relu2, AFT-relu and AFT-softmax, tested on CIFAR10.
comments.explanation: The caption lists only three variants (AFT-relu2, AFT-relu, and AFT-softmax), while the figure legend inside the picture text shows four distinct lines: AFT-relu, AFT-relu2, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. The caption omits the AFT-sigmoid-softmax variant. This inconsistency could mislead readers about the number of conditions tested and which curves correspond to which variant. The text later mentions all four in the legend, but the caption should match for clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguous legend in Figure 2: redundant 'attention' and 'self-attention'
comments.quote: Figure 2 legend: 'attention', 'self-attention', 'aft', 'causal-linear', 'reformer'
comments.explanation: The accompanying text states that the comparison includes 'the self-attention from Transformers' (presumably standard full attention) as one model. Yet the figure legend lists both 'attention' and 'self-attention' as separate entries, which suggests either two different implementations were tested (e.g., vanilla attention vs. some other variant) or there is a labeling mistake. Since the text only describes one standard attention baseline, a careful reader might wonder what 'attention' and 'self-attention' each represent, creating ambiguity in interpreting the runtime and memory benchmarks.

comments.title: Inconsistency in number of nonlinearity variants
comments.quote: We train three versions of AFT, namely AFT-relu2 (Equation 6), AFT-relu (σq = σk = relu), AFT-relu-softmax: (σq = relu, σk = softmax), AFT-sigmoid-softmax (σq = sigmoid, σk = softmax)
comments.explanation: The sentence states 'three versions' but then lists four distinct variants: AFT-relu2, AFT-relu, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. This is a minor numerical inconsistency. The figure and surrounding discussion confirm that four variants are being compared. The authors likely meant 'four' or made an editing error when grouping the variants.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of MLP hidden dimensionality
comments.quote: The hidden layer of the MLP per block has 4 dimensionality of its input.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'has 4 dimensionality of its input' is ambiguous. Standard Transformer MLPs expand the hidden dimension by a factor (typically 4×). The wording suggests a scalar '4' rather than a multiplier of the input dimension. A reader unfamiliar with common practice might misinterpret this as a fixed dimension of 4, which would be implausibly small. The intended meaning is almost certainly '4 times the dimensionality of its input'. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility if the scaling factor is not explicitly stated.

comments.title: Typo: 'casual AFT' should be 'causal AFT'
comments.quote: We formulate the casual AFT as:
comments.explanation: The word 'casual' is used instead of 'causal'. This is a typo that could confuse a reader if not resolved by context. The surrounding discussion clearly refers to causal (autoregressive) attention, so the meaning is clear, but it's an error in terminology.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity: 'direct interaction' claim may be overstated
comments.quote: AFT enables direct interaction of any two elements within the sequence
comments.explanation: The authors claim AFT enables 'direct' interaction between any two elements. However, in AFT, the key-value pairs are pooled globally (sum or cumulative sum) before being combined with the query. This means each query interacts with a pooled representation of all keys/values, not directly with each individual key-value pair. In standard dot-product attention, each query directly attends to each key individually. The phrase 'direct interaction' could mislead readers into thinking AFT preserves pairwise attention weights. While AFT does allow each position to influence the output via the pooling, the interaction is indirect because the query sees a summary. This is a nuanced overclaim. The authors later clarify that the pooling is over the context, but the prose here is ambiguous.

comments.title: Missing definition of notation in recursion formula
comments.quote: we have a simple recursion of Yt = σq(Q′t) ⊙ (σk(K′t)⊙V′t + KVt−1) with KVt =  t t′ =1  σk(Kt′′)⊙Vt′′
comments.explanation: The recursion formula contains garbled characters (e.g., '') likely due to OCR corruption. The intended formula appears to be Y_t = σ_q(Q'_t) ⊙ (σ_k(K'_t)⊙V'_t + KV_{t-1}) with KV_t = Σ_{t'=1}^t (σ_k(K'_{t'})⊙V'_{t'}). The explanation is understandable from context, but the corrupted symbols make it hard to verify the exact intended expression. Since this is likely OCR noise rather than author error, I consider it minor, but it could be confusing.

comments.title: Unclear definition of 'pooled over the context dimension'
comments.quote: the key and value are first combined with an element-wise multiplication, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension, yielding a fixed length context vector ∈ R^d
comments.explanation: The term 'pooled over the context dimension' is ambiguous. Does this mean average pooling, sum pooling, or something else? In the causal case, they later specify a cumulative sum, but for the general (non-causal) case, the type of pooling is not specified. The summary mentions 'global/local pooling' but does not define the operation. A reader might not know whether to use sum, mean, or max pooling. The later equations (e.g., Equation 3) imply a summation, but earlier in the passage, the prose is vague. This could affect reproducibility.

comments.title: Potential error: 'extremely efficiency' is ungrammatical but not technical
comments.quote: The resulting operation has an extremely efficiency of O(Td) w.r.t. both time and space
comments.explanation: Minor language error ('extremely efficiency' should be 'extreme efficiency' or 'extremely efficient'). Not a technical issue.

comments.title: Overclaim: 'first model that achieves linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions'
comments.quote: which is the first model that achieves linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions
comments.explanation: The authors claim AFT is the first model to achieve O(Td) complexity. However, the Linear Transformer (Katharopoulos et al., 2020) also achieves O(Td^2) which is linear in T but quadratic in d. The authors differentiate by noting that AFT is linear in d as well (O(Td) vs O(Td^2)). While this is technically true, the phrase 'first model' may be an overstatement if there exist other models (e.g., lightweight convolutions, gated RNNs) that also achieve O(Td) or better. The authors later acknowledge related work like gated RNNs, but the claim here is bold. Given that AFT is a Transformer variant, the claim may be defensible within that subfamily, but it is worth flagging as potentially overreaching.

comments.quote: wt,t′ =  tt′ ex=1p([exp(] I(t−[I](t[t′] <s[−][t′]) [<s] uT[t)] [v]u tt[T′]) [v] t′[)][, where I(·) is an indicator function

comments.quote: we have a simple recursion of Yt = σq(Q′t) ⊙ (σk(K′t)⊙V′t + KVt−1) with KVt =  t t′ =1  σk(Kt′′)⊙Vt′′

comments.title: Underspecified: 'pooled over the context dimension' in non-causal case
comments.quote: the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension, yielding a fixed length context vector ∈ R^d
comments.explanation: As noted above, the pooling operation is not explicitly defined for the non-causal case. The later causal case uses a cumulative sum, which is a specific pooling. For general (bidirectional) AFT, the pooling could be sum, mean, or max, but the text does not specify. This is an underspecified method detail that could affect reproducibility. The authors assume the reader will infer from the equation (which is missing from the excerpt), but the prose alone is ambiguous.

comments.title: Questionable claim: 'AFT does not need to approximate any MHA counterpart'
comments.quote: AFT does not need to approximate any MHA counterpart and can indeed have very different inductive biases than that of a standard Transformer.
comments.explanation: This statement is true in principle but may be misleading. The authors show that AFT can be viewed as an extreme case of MHA with relu and d_k=1, but they also claim AFT is not generally equivalent. However, the 'different inductive biases' are not formally characterized. The claim is supported by the fact that AFT uses element-wise operations and global pooling, but without empirical or theoretical backing, it reads as speculation. The experiments later show competitive performance, but the claim here is more about design philosophy. It is not a major error, but it is a minor logical leap.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of AFT kernel dimension in Dynamic Convolution comparison
comments.quote: However, AFT operates in an extreme case where the dimension of the kernel is 1 along both the feature and spatial dimensions, again presenting superior efficiency.
comments.explanation: The authors attempt to relate AFT to dynamic convolution, but the description of the kernel's dimensions is incorrect. In AFT, the pooled key–value representation is a vector of dimension d (the feature dimension), not 1. Only the spatial dimension is reduced to 1 due to pooling. Claiming that both dimensions are 1 would imply the kernel is a scalar, which does not match AFT's element-wise multiplication of a d‑dimensional vector with the query. This mischaracterization could confuse readers about AFT's operation and its efficiency claims. The earlier description of AFT's complexity and operation elsewhere in the paper correctly implies a d‑dimensional kernel, so this is an internal inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Figure 4 caption and figure legend
comments.quote: Figure 4: Proof of concept experiments for AFT-relu2, AFT-relu and AFT-softmax, tested on CIFAR10.
comments.explanation: The caption lists only three variants (AFT-relu2, AFT-relu, and AFT-softmax), while the figure legend inside the picture text shows four distinct lines: AFT-relu, AFT-relu2, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. The caption omits the AFT-sigmoid-softmax variant. This inconsistency could mislead readers about the number of conditions tested and which curves correspond to which variant. The text later mentions all four in the legend, but the caption should match for clarity.

comments.title: Multiple typographical errors in Related Work and caption
comments.quote:
1) 'gets rid the need' (should be 'gets rid of the need')
2) 'where the we adopt' (extra 'the')
3) 'which is amendable to' (should be 'amenable')
4) 'more stable and yields significantly better results' (subject-verb agreement: 'yield' for plural subject)
comments.explanation: These errors are unlikely to cause serious confusion but reflect a lack of copy-editing. The missing word in 'gets rid the need' and the extra 'the' in 'where the we adopt' are clear typos. 'Amendable' is not a word in this context; the intended word is 'amenable'. The subject in the last phrase is 'AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax' (plural), so the verb should be 'yield' rather than 'yields'. While minor, they detract from the paper's professionalism.

comments.title: Ambiguous legend in Figure 2: redundant 'attention' and 'self-attention'
comments.quote: Figure 2 legend: 'attention', 'self-attention', 'aft', 'causal-linear', 'reformer'
comments.explanation: The accompanying text states that the comparison includes 'the self-attention from Transformers' (presumably standard full attention) as one model. Yet the figure legend lists both 'attention' and 'self-attention' as separate entries, which suggests either two different implementations were tested (e.g., vanilla attention vs. some other variant) or there is a labeling mistake. Since the text only describes one standard attention baseline, a careful reader might wonder what 'attention' and 'self-attention' each represent, creating ambiguity in interpreting the runtime and memory benchmarks.

comments.title: Inconsistency in number of nonlinearity variants
comments.quote: We train three versions of AFT, namely AFT-relu2 (Equation 6), AFT-relu (σq = σk = relu), AFT-relu-softmax: (σq = relu, σk = softmax), AFT-sigmoid-softmax (σq = sigmoid, σk = softmax)
comments.explanation: The sentence states 'three versions' but then lists four distinct variants: AFT-relu2, AFT-relu, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. This is a minor numerical inconsistency. The figure and surrounding discussion confirm that four variants are being compared. The authors likely meant 'four' or made an editing error when grouping the variants.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of MLP hidden dimensionality
comments.quote: The hidden layer of the MLP per block has 4 dimensionality of its input.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'has 4 dimensionality of its input' is ambiguous. Standard Transformer MLPs expand the hidden dimension by a factor (typically 4×). The wording suggests a scalar '4' rather than a multiplier of the input dimension. A reader unfamiliar with common practice might misinterpret this as a fixed dimension of 4, which would be implausibly small. The intended meaning is almost certainly '4 times the dimensionality of its input'. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility if the scaling factor is not explicitly stated.

comments.title: Typo: 'cae' instead of 'case'
comments.quote: where a transformer block consists of an attention layer (AFT layer in our cae) with residual connection
comments.explanation: The word 'cae' is a typo for 'case'. While minor, it is a clear OCR or typing error that could momentarily confuse the reader. It does not affect the scientific content but should be corrected.
